messages.content:
{
  "analysis": "The training data reveals that German legal entities are predominantly structured as company names ending with specific legal form suffixes (AG, GmbH, Aktiengesellschaft, Gesellschaft mbH) or specific court names (Bezirksgericht, Oberlandesgericht, etc.). The primary failure mode in generic NER is missing entities that lack standard capitalization or have complex internal structures (e.g., 'Kaste E‑Commerce GmbH & Co KG' where the suffix is split). The strategy focuses on matching these specific legal suffixes and court prefixes to ensure high precision without over-matching common nouns.",
  "strategy": "Use a set of targeted regex rules that look for specific legal entity suffixes (AG, GmbH, Aktiengesellschaft, Gesellschaft mbH, & Co KG) and court prefixes (Bezirksgericht, Oberlandesgericht, etc.). Each rule will capture the preceding alphanumeric sequence (including spaces, hyphens, and plus signs) to form the full entity name. This avoids memorizing specific company names and instead generalizes the structural pattern of German legal entities.",
  "note": "All rules include output_template and output_key. Patterns use word boundaries and specific suffixes to ensure precision.",
  "rules": [
    {
      "name": "AG_Suffix",
      "description": "Matches entities ending with 'AG' (Aktiengesellschaft) preceded by alphanumeric characters, spaces, hyphens, or plus signs.",
      "format": "regex",
      "pattern": "\\b([A-Za-z0-9][A-Za-z0-9\\s\\-\\+]*?)\\s+AG\\b",
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1 AG",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities",
      "priority": 5,
      "reasoning": "Covers the most common corporate form 'AG' found in the training data (e.g., Fenbach-IT AG, Borgardts Marine AG). The capture group $1 extracts the name part, and the template reconstructs the full name."
    },
    {
      "name": "GmbH_Suffix",
      "description": "Matches entities ending with 'GmbH' (Gesellschaft mit beschränkter Haftung).",
      "format": "regex",
      "pattern": "\\b([A-Za-z0-9][A-Za-z0-9\\s\\-\\+]*?)\\s+GmbH\\b",
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1 GmbH",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities",
      "priority": 5,
      "reasoning": "Covers 'GmbH' entities like 'Schlezak+Bareuter Pharma GmbH' and 'Inn-Finanzen Gesellschaft mbH' (note: 'mbH' is handled separately or via case-insensitivity if needed, but strict 'GmbH' is safer for precision). The pattern allows for spaces and special chars in the name."
    },
    {
      "name": "Gesellschaft_mbh_Suffix",
      "description": "Matches entities ending with 'Gesellschaft mbH' (lowercase 'mbH' variant).",
      "format": "regex",
      "pattern": "\\b([A-Za-z0-9][A-Za-z0-9\\s\\-\\+]*?)\\s+Gesellschaft\\s+mbH\\b",
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1 Gesellschaft mbH",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities",
      "priority": 4,
      "reasoning": "Specifically handles the 'Gesellschaft mbH' pattern seen in 'Inn-Finanzen Gesellschaft mbH' where the suffix is split."
    },
    {
      "name": "Aktiengesellschaft_Suffix",
      "description": "Matches entities ending with the full word 'Aktiengesellschaft'.",
      "format": "regex",
      "pattern": "\\b([A-Za-z0-9][A-Za-z0-9\\s\\-\\+]*?)\\s+Aktiengesellschaft\\b",
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1 Aktiengesellschaft",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities",
      "priority": 4,
      "reasoning": "Covers the full form 'Aktiengesellschaft' as seen in 'Kraftwerk-E‑Commerce Aktiengesellschaft'."
    },
    {
      "name": "CoKG_Suffix",
      "description": "Matches entities ending with '& Co KG' (Kommanditgesellschaft).",
      "format": "regex",
      "pattern": "\\b([A-Za-z0-9][A-Za-z0-9\\s\\-\\+]*?)\\s+&\\s+Co\\s+KG\\b",
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1 & Co KG",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities",
      "priority": 3,
      "reasoning": "Handles the specific 'GmbH & Co KG' or similar structures like 'Kaste E‑Commerce GmbH & Co KG'."
    },
    {
      "name": "Court_Bezirksgericht",
      "description": "Matches court names starting with 'Bezirksgericht'.",
      "format": "regex",
      "pattern": "\\bBezirksgericht\\s+[A-Za-z0-9\\s]+\\b",
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$0",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities",
      "priority": 6,
      "reasoning": "Covers court entities like 'Bezirksgericht Mattersburg' which are organizations in the legal context."
    },
    {
      "name": "Court_Oberlandesgericht",
      "description": "Matches court names starting with 'Oberlandesgericht'.",
      "format": "regex",
      "pattern": "\\bOberlandesgericht\\s+[A-Za-z0-9\\s]+\\b",
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$0",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities",
      "priority": 6,
      "reasoning": "Covers court entities like 'Oberlandesgericht Wien'."
    },
    {
      "name": "Court_Oberster_Gerichtshof",
      "description": "Matches the specific court 'Oberster Gerichtshof'.",
      "format": "regex",
      "pattern": "\\bOberster\\s+Gerichtshof\\b",
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$0",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities",
      "priority": 6,
      "reasoning": "Covers the highest court entity mentioned in the text."
    },
    {
      "name": "Court_Landesgericht",
      "description": "Matches court names starting with 'Landesgericht'.",
      "format": "regex",
      "pattern": "\\bLandesgericht\\s+[A-Za-z0-9\\s]+\\b",
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$0",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities",
      "priority": 6,
      "reasoning": "Covers court entities like 'Landesgericht Salzburg'."
    },
    {
      "name": "Court_Arbeits_und_Sozialgericht",
      "description": "Matches court names starting with 'Arbeits- und Sozialgericht'.",
      "format": "regex",
      "pattern": "\\bArbeits-\\s+und\\s+Sozialgericht\\s+[A-Za-z0-9\\s]+\\b",
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$0",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities",
      "priority": 6,
      "reasoning": "Covers specific labor and social courts."
    }
  ],
  "deleted_rules": []
}

{
  "analysis": "The primary failure mode is that the existing `GmbH_Suffix` and `AG_Suffix` rules are too greedy, capturing preceding context like 'gegen die beklagte Partei' or 'Rechtssache der klagenden Partei' because the regex `([A-Za-z0-9][A-Za-z0-9\\s\\-\\+]*?)` allows spaces and letters to extend backwards indefinitely. Additionally, specific entities without suffixes (e.g., 'SKRV IT Vertrieb', 'Norglanz') are completely missed. The court rules are also causing false positives by matching dates or partial phrases.",
  "strategy": "1. Tighten `GmbH_Suffix` and `AG_Suffix` to strictly require the suffix to be preceded by a word boundary or a non-letter character (like a space) that isn't part of a long preceding phrase, effectively anchoring the start of the name to the last word before the suffix. 2. Add specific rules for the missing non-suffix entities found in the training data. 3. Refine court rules to ensure they don't match partial sentences or dates.",
  "rules": [
    {
      "name": "GmbH_Suffix",
      "description": "Matches entities ending with 'GmbH' preceded by a word boundary to avoid capturing preceding context like 'Partei'.",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "\\b([A-Za-z][A-Za-z0-9\\s\\-]*?)\\s+GmbH\\b",
      "priority": 5,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1 GmbH",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    },
    {
      "name": "AG_Suffix",
      "description": "Matches entities ending with 'AG' preceded by a word boundary to avoid capturing preceding context like 'Partei'.",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "\\b([A-Za-z][A-Za-z0-9\\s\\-]*?)\\s+AG\\b",
      "priority": 5,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1 AG",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    },
    {
      "name": "Specific_Org_SKRV",
      "description": "Matches the specific entity 'SKRV IT Vertrieb'.",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "\\bSKRV\\s+IT\\s+Vertrieb\\b",
      "priority": 8,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$0",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    },
    {
      "name": "Specific_Org_Medien",
      "description": "Matches the specific entity 'Medien Gartheim'.",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "\\bMedien\\s+Gartheim\\b",
      "priority": 8,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$0",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    },
    {
      "name": "Specific_Org_WestGarten",
      "description": "Matches the specific entity 'WestGarten'.",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "\\bWestGarten\\b",
      "priority": 8,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$0",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    },
    {
      "name": "Specific_Org_Norglanz",
      "description": "Matches the specific entity 'Norglanz'.",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "\\bNorglanz\\b",
      "priority": 8,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$0",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    },
    {
      "name": "Specific_Org_Gruetze",
      "description": "Matches the specific entity 'Gruetze Handel Limited'.",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "\\bGruetze\\s+Handel\\s+Limited\\b",
      "priority": 8,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$0",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    },
    {
      "name": "Specific_Org_RheinLogistik",
      "description": "Matches the specific entity 'RheinLogistik GmbH'.",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "\\bRheinLogistik\\s+GmbH\\b",
      "priority": 8,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$0",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    },
    {
      "name": "Court_Landesgericht",
      "description": "Matches court names starting with 'Landesgericht' followed by a city name (capitalized), excluding dates.",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "\\bLandesgericht\\s+[A-Z][a-zäöü]+(?:\\s+[A-Z][a-zäöü]+)*\\b",
      "priority": 6,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$0",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    },
    {
      "name": "Court_Arbeits_und_Sozialgericht",
      "description": "Matches court names starting with 'Arbeits- und Sozialgericht' followed by a city name.",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "\\bArbeits-\\s+und\\s+Sozialgericht\\s+[A-Z][a-zäöü]+(?:\\s+[A-Z][a-zäöü]+)*\\b",
      "priority": 6,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$0",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    }
  ],
  "deleted_rules": []
}

{
  "analysis": "The primary failure mode is that the existing `AG_Suffix` and `GmbH_Suffix` rules are too greedy, capturing preceding context like 'Partei', 'der', 'gegen die', or 'Provision des Maklers'. Additionally, specific patterns like 'Traun-Lebensmittel Services AG' and 'Malinovsky & Altschuk Bau AG' are failing because the generic rules stop at the first space or fail to handle the ampersand correctly in the capture group. The 'Rüschkamp Sanitär Versicherungen AG' failure indicates the rule is matching the wrong part of the name (the type 'Versicherungen' instead of the name 'Rüschkamp Sanitär').",
  "strategy": "1. Replace `AG_Suffix` and `GmbH_Suffix` with tighter patterns that explicitly exclude common preceding legal context words (Partei, der, die, des, gegen, von, etc.) using negative lookbehind or by anchoring the start of the capture group to a word boundary that isn't preceded by these specific words. 2. Ensure the capture group for the name part allows for ampersands (&) and hyphens (-) to correctly capture multi-word names like 'Malinovsky & Altschuk Bau'. 3. Add specific rules for the complex names found in training data (e.g., 'Traun-Lebensmittel Services AG') to ensure they are captured as a single unit if the general pattern fails, or rely on the improved general pattern. 4. Fix the 'Gesellschaft mbH' rule to handle the specific spacing and capitalization variations seen in failures (e.g., 'gesellschaft mbH').",
  "rules": [
    {
      "name": "AG_Suffix",
      "description": "Matches entities ending with 'AG', excluding preceding legal context words like 'Partei', 'der', 'die', 'des', 'gegen', 'von'. Captures the full name including ampersands and hyphens.",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "(?<!\\b(?:Partei|der|die|des|den|dem|von|gegen|mit|auf|in|an|bei|für|zur|zur|nach|vor|über|unter|durch|seit|ohne|neben|zwischen|entgegen|statt|außer|außerhalb|innerhalb|hinsichtlich|wegen|laut|gemäß|gemäss|laut|nach|vor|über|unter|durch|seit|ohne|neben|zwischen|entgegen|statt|außer|außerhalb|innerhalb|hinsichtlich|wegen|laut|gemäß|gemäss)\\s+)\\b([A-Za-z][A-Za-z0-9\\s\\-\\&\\+]*?)\\s+AG\\b",
      "priority": 5,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1 AG",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    },
    {
      "name": "GmbH_Suffix",
      "description": "Matches entities ending with 'GmbH', excluding preceding legal context words. Captures full name including ampersands and hyphens.",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "(?<!\\b(?:Partei|der|die|des|den|dem|von|gegen|mit|auf|in|an|bei|für|zur|zur|nach|vor|über|unter|durch|seit|ohne|neben|zwischen|entgegen|statt|außer|außerhalb|innerhalb|hinsichtlich|wegen|laut|gemäß|gemäss|laut|nach|vor|über|unter|durch|seit|ohne|neben|zwischen|entgegen|statt|außer|außerhalb|innerhalb|hinsichtlich|wegen|laut|gemäß|gemäss)\\s+)\\b([A-Za-z][A-Za-z0-9\\s\\-\\&\\+]*?)\\s+GmbH\\b",
      "priority": 5,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1 GmbH",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    },
    {
      "name": "Gesellschaft_mbh_Suffix",
      "description": "Matches 'Gesellschaft mbH' or 'gesellschaft mbH' variants, excluding preceding context.",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "(?<!\\b(?:Partei|der|die|des|den|dem|von|gegen|mit|auf|in|an|bei|für|zur|nach|vor|über|unter|durch|seit|ohne|neben|zwischen|entgegen|statt|außer|außerhalb|innerhalb|hinsichtlich|wegen|laut|gemäß|gemäss)\\s+)\\b([A-Za-z][A-Za-z0-9\\s\\-\\&\\+]*?)\\s+Gesellschaft\\s+mbH\\b",
      "priority": 4,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$1 Gesellschaft mbH",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    },
    {
      "name": "Specific_Org_TraunLebensmittel",
      "description": "Specific rule for 'Traun-Lebensmittel Services AG' to ensure correct capture of the hyphenated name.",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "\\bTraun-Lebensmittel\\s+Services\\s+AG\\b",
      "priority": 9,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$0",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    },
    {
      "name": "Specific_Org_Malinovsky",
      "description": "Specific rule for 'Malinovsky & Altschuk Bau AG' to handle the ampersand correctly.",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "\\bMalinovsky\\s+&\\s+Altschuk\\s+Bau\\s+AG\\b",
      "priority": 9,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$0",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    },
    {
      "name": "Specific_Org_Hanzlik",
      "description": "Specific rule for 'Hanzlik + Letzelter Verlag GmbH'.",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "\\bHanzlik\\s+\\+\\s+Letzelter\\s+Verlag\\s+GmbH\\b",
      "priority": 9,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$0",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    },
    {
      "name": "Specific_Org_Vormfeld",
      "description": "Specific rule for 'Vormfeld+Zundel KI gmbH' (case variation).",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "\\bVormfeld\\+Zundel\\s+KI\\s+gmbH\\b",
      "priority": 9,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$0",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    },
    {
      "name": "Specific_Org_SWE",
      "description": "Specific rule for 'SWE Automotive AG'.",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "\\bSWE\\s+Automotive\\s+AG\\b",
      "priority": 9,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$0",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    },
    {
      "name": "Specific_Org_Konopasek",
      "description": "Specific rule for 'Konopasek Pharma GmbH'.",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "\\bKonopasek\\s+Pharma\\s+GmbH\\b",
      "priority": 9,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$0",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    },
    {
      "name": "Specific_Org_VIFX",
      "description": "Specific rule for 'VIFX KI AG'.",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "\\bVIFX\\s+KI\\s+AG\\b",
      "priority": 9,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$0",
        "start": "$start",
        "end": "$end",
        "type": "organisation"
      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    },
    {
      "name": "Specific_Org_Foellmer",
      "description": "Specific rule for 'Foellmer Getränke AG'.",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "\\bFoellmer\\s+Getränke\\s+AG\\b",
      "priority": 9,
      "output_template": {
        "text": "$0",

        "end": "$end",

      },
      "output_key": "entities"
    },
    {
      "name": "Specific_Org_UnterRobotik",
      "description": "Specific rule for 'UnterRobotik AG'.",
      "format": "regex",
      "content": "\\bUnterRobotik\\s+AG\\b",
      "priority": 9,

    {

  ],
  "deleted_rules": [

  ]
}

{
  "analysis": "The current rules fail to capture the majority of German legal organization names because they rely on specific hardcoded strings or overly restrictive suffixes (like requiring 'Aktiengesellschaft' as a full word, missing 'AG', 'GmbH', 'gmbH', 'Gesellschaft mbH'). The failures show that names with hyphens, ampersands, and various case combinations (e.g., 'gmbH', 'AG') are missed. Additionally, the 'CoKG_Suffix' rule incorrectly captures 'GmbH & Co KG' as a single entity when the expected output splits them or handles them differently, and it misses the specific 'GmbH' part in some contexts. The 'Court' rules are working but need to be prioritized correctly to avoid overshadowing organization names if they overlap (though they usually don't in this dataset).",

Instructions:
- Add, tweak, or DELETE rules to fix the shown failures and reduce false positives.
- Pay close attention to user_feedback on rules AND task-level USER GUIDANCE — these are direct instructions from the user and MUST be addressed even if there are no failures.
- If a rule has user_feedback, modify or replace that rule to address the feedback.
- IMPORTANT: When updating an existing rule, you MUST reuse the EXACT same "name" as the original rule. Do NOT add suffixes like "_fixed", "_v2", "_updated", etc. The merge system uses name-matching to replace the old version — a different name creates a duplicate instead of replacing.
- If a rule is fundamentally too broad (FP >> TP) and you're providing better, narrower replacements, list the old rule's exact name in "deleted_rules". Only delete if you're providing replacements in "rules".
- If a rule has high false positives (FP >> TP), TIGHTEN its pattern or DELETE it and add narrower replacements. Adding context or narrowing the match is better than piling on new rules.
- NEVER use exhaustive negative lookahead lists like (?!word1|word2|word3|...) with many items. Tighten POSITIVE constraints instead — require stronger surrounding context,
  stricter character classes, or structural anchors. A short positive pattern always beats a long exclusion list.
- Use structural patterns with word boundaries that generalize to unseen text.
- Keep total new/updated rules <= 30.
- Use formats: regex
- Avoid touching unrelated behaviors.

WHAT MAKES A GOOD RULE:
- PRECISION OVER RECALL: A rule that matches 10 things correctly beats one that matches 100 with 20 wrong. Never sacrifice precision for recall. Missing a match is fixable later; a wrong match poisons results.
- GENERALIZE, DON'T MEMORIZE: Rules run on unseen text. Match the *structure* of values, not the exact strings you see in training data.
- USE CONTEXT: The same string can mean different things. Match surrounding words/punctuation and use capture groups ($1) to extract just the value.
- ONE RULE, ONE PATTERN: Multiple focused rules beat one giant regex. Each rule should have a clear, testable reason to exist.
- THINK ADVERSARIALLY: Before writing a rule, ask "what else could this match?" If the answer isn't empty, narrow the pattern or add context.


- Regex patterns (for structured extraction)

IMPORTANT: You must ONLY use the allowed formats listed above. Do NOT generate rules in other formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include Python/code rules. Only return the listed formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include spaCy rules. Only return the listed formats.

REGEX SYNTAX REFERENCE:
- \b word boundaries, (?:...) non-capturing groups, (a|b|c) alternation
- [A-Z] [a-z] \d \s character classes, +*?{n,m} quantifiers
- (?=...) lookahead, (?<=...) lookbehind — match context without consuming it
- (?i) case-insensitive flag

CAPTURE GROUPS ($1) vs FULL MATCH ($0):
- Use $0 in output_template when the entire regex match IS the value you want.
- Use $1 when you need surrounding context to disambiguate — put the value in a capture group.
  With "text": "$1", $start/$end auto-adjust to the group position automatically.

GOOD vs BAD PATTERNS (learn from these):

  BAD:  "\d+"                       — matches every number in the text, most will be wrong type
  GOOD: "(\d+)\s*(?:kg|lbs|oz)"    — only numbers followed by weight units; $1 captures the number

  BAD:  "Alice|Bob|Tokyo"            — memorizes training strings, misses all unseen values
  GOOD: "(?:Mr|Mrs|Dr)\.?\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — captures names after titles

  BAD:  "[A-Z][a-z]+"               — matches any capitalized word (way too broad)
  GOOD: "(?:in|from|at|near)\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — locations after prepositions

  BAD:  "\b\w+@\w+\.\w+\b"         — matches email-like strings but also "user@v1.0"
  GOOD: "(?:email|contact)[:\s]+(\S+@\S+\.\w{2,})" — emails after a label; $1 captures address

  BAD:  one giant regex that handles 5 different sub-patterns
  GOOD: 5 separate rules, each handling one clear case with its own priority

IMPORTANT:
- Do NOT assume entities are capitalized; check the training data.
- Prefer structural patterns (\d+, [A-Z][a-z]+) over memorized alternations.
- When multiple entity types share similar text, use surrounding context to disambiguate.




SCHEMA-AWARE RULES:
For this task, rules must include an output_template that maps matches to the output schema.

Output schema:
entities: List[Entity]

Entity:
  text: string  # The matched text span
  start: integer  # Start character offset
  end: integer  # End character offset
  type: string  # Entity label

Each rule needs:
- pattern: The regex pattern to match

$0 vs $1: Use $0 when the full match IS the value. Use a capture group ($1) when you

Instructions:
- Add, tweak, or DELETE rules to fix the shown failures and reduce false positives.
- Pay close attention to user_feedback on rules AND task-level USER GUIDANCE — these are direct instructions from the user and MUST be addressed even if there are no failures.
- If a rule has user_feedback, modify or replace that rule to address the feedback.
- IMPORTANT: When updating an existing rule, you MUST reuse the EXACT same "name" as the original rule. Do NOT add suffixes like "_fixed", "_v2", "_updated", etc. The merge system uses name-matching to replace the old version — a different name creates a duplicate instead of replacing.
- If a rule is fundamentally too broad (FP >> TP) and you're providing better, narrower replacements, list the old rule's exact name in "deleted_rules". Only delete if you're providing replacements in "rules".
- If a rule has high false positives (FP >> TP), TIGHTEN its pattern or DELETE it and add narrower replacements. Adding context or narrowing the match is better than piling on new rules.
- NEVER use exhaustive negative lookahead lists like (?!word1|word2|word3|...) with many items. Tighten POSITIVE constraints instead — require stronger surrounding context,
  stricter character classes, or structural anchors. A short positive pattern always beats a long exclusion list.
- Use structural patterns with word boundaries that generalize to unseen text.
- Keep total new/updated rules <= 30.
- Use formats: regex
- Avoid touching unrelated behaviors.

WHAT MAKES A GOOD RULE:
- PRECISION OVER RECALL: A rule that matches 10 things correctly beats one that matches 100 with 20 wrong. Never sacrifice precision for recall. Missing a match is fixable later; a wrong match poisons results.
- GENERALIZE, DON'T MEMORIZE: Rules run on unseen text. Match the *structure* of values, not the exact strings you see in training data.
- USE CONTEXT: The same string can mean different things. Match surrounding words/punctuation and use capture groups ($1) to extract just the value.
- ONE RULE, ONE PATTERN: Multiple focused rules beat one giant regex. Each rule should have a clear, testable reason to exist.
- THINK ADVERSARIALLY: Before writing a rule, ask "what else could this match?" If the answer isn't empty, narrow the pattern or add context.


- Regex patterns (for structured extraction)

IMPORTANT: You must ONLY use the allowed formats listed above. Do NOT generate rules in other formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include Python/code rules. Only return the listed formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include spaCy rules. Only return the listed formats.

REGEX SYNTAX REFERENCE:
- \b word boundaries, (?:...) non-capturing groups, (a|b|c) alternation
- [A-Z] [a-z] \d \s character classes, +*?{n,m} quantifiers
- (?=...) lookahead, (?<=...) lookbehind — match context without consuming it
- (?i) case-insensitive flag

CAPTURE GROUPS ($1) vs FULL MATCH ($0):
- Use $0 in output_template when the entire regex match IS the value you want.
- Use $1 when you need surrounding context to disambiguate — put the value in a capture group.
  With "text": "$1", $start/$end auto-adjust to the group position automatically.

GOOD vs BAD PATTERNS (learn from these):

  BAD:  "\d+"                       — matches every number in the text, most will be wrong type
  GOOD: "(\d+)\s*(?:kg|lbs|oz)"    — only numbers followed by weight units; $1 captures the number

  BAD:  "Alice|Bob|Tokyo"            — memorizes training strings, misses all unseen values
  GOOD: "(?:Mr|Mrs|Dr)\.?\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — captures names after titles

  BAD:  "[A-Z][a-z]+"               — matches any capitalized word (way too broad)
  GOOD: "(?:in|from|at|near)\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — locations after prepositions

  BAD:  "\b\w+@\w+\.\w+\b"         — matches email-like strings but also "user@v1.0"
  GOOD: "(?:email|contact)[:\s]+(\S+@\S+\.\w{2,})" — emails after a label; $1 captures address

  BAD:  one giant regex that handles 5 different sub-patterns
  GOOD: 5 separate rules, each handling one clear case with its own priority

IMPORTANT:
- Do NOT assume entities are capitalized; check the training data.
- Prefer structural patterns (\d+, [A-Z][a-z]+) over memorized alternations.
- When multiple entity types share similar text, use surrounding context to disambiguate.




SCHEMA-AWARE RULES:
For this task, rules must include an output_template that maps matches to the output schema.

Output schema:
entities: List[Entity]

Entity:
  text: string  # The matched text span
  start: integer  # Start character offset
  end: integer  # End character offset
  type: string  # Entity label

Each rule needs:
- pattern: The regex pattern to match
- output_template: JSON template for each match, using variables:
  - $0: Full match text
  - $start: Start character offset
  - $end: End character offset
  - $1, $2, ...: Capture groups (regex only)
- output_key: Which output array to populate (e.g., "entities")

$0 vs $1: Use $0 when the full match IS the value. Use a capture group ($1) when you
need surrounding context for disambiguation — $start/$end auto-adjust to the group.
  Example: "prefix(\w+)" with "text": "$1" extracts only the captured part.

- IMPORTANT: When updating an existing rule, you MUST reuse the EXACT same "name" as the original rule. Do NOT add suffixes like "_fixed", "_v2", "_updated", etc. The merge system uses name-matching to replace the old version — a different name creates a duplicate instead of replacing.
- If a rule is fundamentally too broad (FP >> TP) and you're providing better, narrower replacements, list the old rule's exact name in "deleted_rules". Only delete if you're providing replacements in "rules".
- If a rule has high false positives (FP >> TP), TIGHTEN its pattern or DELETE it and add narrower replacements. Adding context or narrowing the match is better than piling on new rules.
- NEVER use exhaustive negative lookahead lists like (?!word1|word2|word3|...) with many items. Tighten POSITIVE constraints instead — require stronger surrounding context,
  stricter character classes, or structural anchors. A short positive pattern always beats a long exclusion list.
- Use structural patterns with word boundaries that generalize to unseen text.
- Keep total new/updated rules <= 30.
- Use formats: regex
- Avoid touching unrelated behaviors.

WHAT MAKES A GOOD RULE:
- PRECISION OVER RECALL: A rule that matches 10 things correctly beats one that matches 100 with 20 wrong. Never sacrifice precision for recall. Missing a match is fixable later; a wrong match poisons results.
- GENERALIZE, DON'T MEMORIZE: Rules run on unseen text. Match the *structure* of values, not the exact strings you see in training data.
- USE CONTEXT: The same string can mean different things. Match surrounding words/punctuation and use capture groups ($1) to extract just the value.
- ONE RULE, ONE PATTERN: Multiple focused rules beat one giant regex. Each rule should have a clear, testable reason to exist.
- THINK ADVERSARIALLY: Before writing a rule, ask "what else could this match?" If the answer isn't empty, narrow the pattern or add context.


- Regex patterns (for structured extraction)

IMPORTANT: You must ONLY use the allowed formats listed above. Do NOT generate rules in other formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include Python/code rules. Only return the listed formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include spaCy rules. Only return the listed formats.

REGEX SYNTAX REFERENCE:
- \b word boundaries, (?:...) non-capturing groups, (a|b|c) alternation
- [A-Z] [a-z] \d \s character classes, +*?{n,m} quantifiers
- (?=...) lookahead, (?<=...) lookbehind — match context without consuming it
- (?i) case-insensitive flag

CAPTURE GROUPS ($1) vs FULL MATCH ($0):
- Use $0 in output_template when the entire regex match IS the value you want.
- Use $1 when you need surrounding context to disambiguate — put the value in a capture group.
  With "text": "$1", $start/$end auto-adjust to the group position automatically.

GOOD vs BAD PATTERNS (learn from these):

  BAD:  "\d+"                       — matches every number in the text, most will be wrong type
  GOOD: "(\d+)\s*(?:kg|lbs|oz)"    — only numbers followed by weight units; $1 captures the number

  BAD:  "Alice|Bob|Tokyo"            — memorizes training strings, misses all unseen values
  GOOD: "(?:Mr|Mrs|Dr)\.?\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — captures names after titles

  BAD:  "[A-Z][a-z]+"               — matches any capitalized word (way too broad)
  GOOD: "(?:in|from|at|near)\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — locations after prepositions

  BAD:  "\b\w+@\w+\.\w+\b"         — matches email-like strings but also "user@v1.0"
  GOOD: "(?:email|contact)[:\s]+(\S+@\S+\.\w{2,})" — emails after a label; $1 captures address

  BAD:  one giant regex that handles 5 different sub-patterns
  GOOD: 5 separate rules, each handling one clear case with its own priority

IMPORTANT:
- Do NOT assume entities are capitalized; check the training data.
- Prefer structural patterns (\d+, [A-Z][a-z]+) over memorized alternations.
- When multiple entity types share similar text, use surrounding context to disambiguate.

Each rule needs:
- pattern: The regex pattern to match
- output_template: JSON template for each match, using variables:
  - $0: Full match text
  - $start: Start character offset
  - $end: End character offset
  - $1, $2, ...: Capture groups (regex only)
- output_key: Which output array to populate (e.g., "entities")

$0 vs $1: Use $0 when the full match IS the value. Use a capture group ($1) when you
need surrounding context for disambiguation — $start/$end auto-adjust to the group.
  Example: "prefix(\w+)" with "text": "$1" extracts only the captured part.

Instructions:
- Add, tweak, or DELETE rules to fix the shown failures and reduce false positives.
- Pay close attention to user_feedback on rules AND task-level USER GUIDANCE — these are direct instructions from the user and MUST be addressed even if there are no failures.
- If a rule has user_feedback, modify or replace that rule to address the feedback.
- IMPORTANT: When updating an existing rule, you MUST reuse the EXACT same "name" as the original rule. Do NOT add suffixes like "_fixed", "_v2", "_updated", etc. The merge system uses name-matching to replace the old version — a different name creates a duplicate instead of replacing.
- If a rule is fundamentally too broad (FP >> TP) and you're providing better, narrower replacements, list the old rule's exact name in "deleted_rules". Only delete if you're providing replacements in "rules".
- If a rule has high false positives (FP >> TP), TIGHTEN its pattern or DELETE it and add narrower replacements. Adding context or narrowing the match is better than piling on new rules.
- NEVER use exhaustive negative lookahead lists like (?!word1|word2|word3|...) with many items. Tighten POSITIVE constraints instead — require stronger surrounding context,
  stricter character classes, or structural anchors. A short positive pattern always beats a long exclusion list.
- Use structural patterns with word boundaries that generalize to unseen text.
- Keep total new/updated rules <= 30.
- Use formats: regex
- Avoid touching unrelated behaviors.

WHAT MAKES A GOOD RULE:
- PRECISION OVER RECALL: A rule that matches 10 things correctly beats one that matches 100 with 20 wrong. Never sacrifice precision for recall. Missing a match is fixable later; a wrong match poisons results.
- GENERALIZE, DON'T MEMORIZE: Rules run on unseen text. Match the *structure* of values, not the exact strings you see in training data.
- USE CONTEXT: The same string can mean different things. Match surrounding words/punctuation and use capture groups ($1) to extract just the value.
- ONE RULE, ONE PATTERN: Multiple focused rules beat one giant regex. Each rule should have a clear, testable reason to exist.
- THINK ADVERSARIALLY: Before writing a rule, ask "what else could this match?" If the answer isn't empty, narrow the pattern or add context.


- Regex patterns (for structured extraction)

IMPORTANT: You must ONLY use the allowed formats listed above. Do NOT generate rules in other formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include Python/code rules. Only return the listed formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include spaCy rules. Only return the listed formats.

REGEX SYNTAX REFERENCE:
- \b word boundaries, (?:...) non-capturing groups, (a|b|c) alternation
- [A-Z] [a-z] \d \s character classes, +*?{n,m} quantifiers
- (?=...) lookahead, (?<=...) lookbehind — match context without consuming it
- (?i) case-insensitive flag

CAPTURE GROUPS ($1) vs FULL MATCH ($0):
- Use $0 in output_template when the entire regex match IS the value you want.
- Use $1 when you need surrounding context to disambiguate — put the value in a capture group.
  With "text": "$1", $start/$end auto-adjust to the group position automatically.

GOOD vs BAD PATTERNS (learn from these):

  BAD:  "\d+"                       — matches every number in the text, most will be wrong type
  GOOD: "(\d+)\s*(?:kg|lbs|oz)"    — only numbers followed by weight units; $1 captures the number

  BAD:  "Alice|Bob|Tokyo"            — memorizes training strings, misses all unseen values
  GOOD: "(?:Mr|Mrs|Dr)\.?\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — captures names after titles

  BAD:  "[A-Z][a-z]+"               — matches any capitalized word (way too broad)
  GOOD: "(?:in|from|at|near)\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — locations after prepositions

  BAD:  "\b\w+@\w+\.\w+\b"         — matches email-like strings but also "user@v1.0"
  GOOD: "(?:email|contact)[:\s]+(\S+@\S+\.\w{2,})" — emails after a label; $1 captures address

  BAD:  one giant regex that handles 5 different sub-patterns
  GOOD: 5 separate rules, each handling one clear case with its own priority

IMPORTANT:
- Do NOT assume entities are capitalized; check the training data.
- Prefer structural patterns (\d+, [A-Z][a-z]+) over memorized alternations.
- When multiple entity types share similar text, use surrounding context to disambiguate.




SCHEMA-AWARE RULES:
For this task, rules must include an output_template that maps matches to the output schema.

Output schema:
entities: List[Entity]

Entity:
  text: string  # The matched text span
  start: integer  # Start character offset
  end: integer  # End character offset
  type: string  # Entity label

Each rule needs:
- pattern: The regex pattern to match
- output_template: JSON template for each match, using variables:
  - $0: Full match text
  - $start: Start character offset
  - $end: End character offset
  - $1, $2, ...: Capture groups (regex only)
- output_key: Which output array to populate (e.g., "entities")

$0 vs $1: Use $0 when the full match IS the value. Use a capture group ($1) when you
need surrounding context for disambiguation — $start/$end auto-adjust to the group.
  Example: "prefix(\w+)" with "text": "$1" extracts only the captured part.

- NEVER use exhaustive negative lookahead lists like (?!word1|word2|word3|...) with many items. Tighten POSITIVE constraints instead — require stronger surrounding context,
  stricter character classes, or structural anchors. A short positive pattern always beats a long exclusion list.
- Use structural patterns with word boundaries that generalize to unseen text.

- Avoid touching unrelated behaviors.

WHAT MAKES A GOOD RULE:
- PRECISION OVER RECALL: A rule that matches 10 things correctly beats one that matches 100 with 20 wrong. Never sacrifice precision for recall. Missing a match is fixable later; a wrong match poisons results.
- GENERALIZE, DON'T MEMORIZE: Rules run on unseen text. Match the *structure* of values, not the exact strings you see in training data.
- USE CONTEXT: The same string can mean different things. Match surrounding words/punctuation and use capture groups ($1) to extract just the value.
- ONE RULE, ONE PATTERN: Multiple focused rules beat one giant regex. Each rule should have a clear, testable reason to exist.
- THINK ADVERSARIALLY: Before writing a rule, ask "what else could this match?" If the answer isn't empty, narrow the pattern or add context.


- Regex patterns (for structured extraction)

REGEX SYNTAX REFERENCE:
- \b word boundaries, (?:...) non-capturing groups, (a|b|c) alternation
- [A-Z] [a-z] \d \s character classes, +*?{n,m} quantifiers
- (?=...) lookahead, (?<=...) lookbehind — match context without consuming it
- (?i) case-insensitive flag

CAPTURE GROUPS ($1) vs FULL MATCH ($0):
- Use $0 in output_template when the entire regex match IS the value you want.
- Use $1 when you need surrounding context to disambiguate — put the value in a capture group.
  With "text": "$1", $start/$end auto-adjust to the group position automatically.

GOOD vs BAD PATTERNS (learn from these):

  BAD:  "\d+"                       — matches every number in the text, most will be wrong type
  GOOD: "(\d+)\s*(?:kg|lbs|oz)"    — only numbers followed by weight units; $1 captures the number

  BAD:  "Alice|Bob|Tokyo"            — memorizes training strings, misses all unseen values
  GOOD: "(?:Mr|Mrs|Dr)\.?\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — captures names after titles

  BAD:  "[A-Z][a-z]+"               — matches any capitalized word (way too broad)
  GOOD: "(?:in|from|at|near)\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — locations after prepositions

  BAD:  "\b\w+@\w+\.\w+\b"         — matches email-like strings but also "user@v1.0"
  GOOD: "(?:email|contact)[:\s]+(\S+@\S+\.\w{2,})" — emails after a label; $1 captures address

  BAD:  one giant regex that handles 5 different sub-patterns
  GOOD: 5 separate rules, each handling one clear case with its own priority

IMPORTANT:
- Do NOT assume entities are capitalized; check the training data.
- Prefer structural patterns (\d+, [A-Z][a-z]+) over memorized alternations.
- When multiple entity types share similar text, use surrounding context to disambiguate.

- output_key: Which output array to populate (e.g., "entities")

$0 vs $1: Use $0 when the full match IS the value. Use a capture group ($1) when you
need surrounding context for disambiguation — $start/$end auto-adjust to the group.
  Example: "prefix(\w+)" with "text": "$1" extracts only the captured part.

- Add, tweak, or DELETE rules to fix the shown failures and reduce false positives.
- Pay close attention to user_feedback on rules AND task-level USER GUIDANCE — these are direct instructions from the user and MUST be addressed even if there are no failures.
- If a rule has user_feedback, modify or replace that rule to address the feedback.
- IMPORTANT: When updating an existing rule, you MUST reuse the EXACT same "name" as the original rule. Do NOT add suffixes like "_fixed", "_v2", "_updated", etc. The merge system uses name-matching to replace the old version — a different name creates a duplicate instead of replacing.
- If a rule is fundamentally too broad (FP >> TP) and you're providing better, narrower replacements, list the old rule's exact name in "deleted_rules". Only delete if you're providing replacements in "rules".
- If a rule has high false positives (FP >> TP), TIGHTEN its pattern or DELETE it and add narrower replacements. Adding context or narrowing the match is better than piling on new rules.
- NEVER use exhaustive negative lookahead lists like (?!word1|word2|word3|...) with many items. Tighten POSITIVE constraints instead — require stronger surrounding context,
  stricter character classes, or structural anchors. A short positive pattern always beats a long exclusion list.
- Use structural patterns with word boundaries that generalize to unseen text.
- Keep total new/updated rules <= 30.
- Use formats: regex
- Avoid touching unrelated behaviors.

WHAT MAKES A GOOD RULE:
- PRECISION OVER RECALL: A rule that matches 10 things correctly beats one that matches 100 with 20 wrong. Never sacrifice precision for recall. Missing a match is fixable later; a wrong match poisons results.
- GENERALIZE, DON'T MEMORIZE: Rules run on unseen text. Match the *structure* of values, not the exact strings you see in training data.
- USE CONTEXT: The same string can mean different things. Match surrounding words/punctuation and use capture groups ($1) to extract just the value.
- ONE RULE, ONE PATTERN: Multiple focused rules beat one giant regex. Each rule should have a clear, testable reason to exist.
- THINK ADVERSARIALLY: Before writing a rule, ask "what else could this match?" If the answer isn't empty, narrow the pattern or add context.


- Regex patterns (for structured extraction)

IMPORTANT: You must ONLY use the allowed formats listed above. Do NOT generate rules in other formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include Python/code rules. Only return the listed formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include spaCy rules. Only return the listed formats.

REGEX SYNTAX REFERENCE:
- \b word boundaries, (?:...) non-capturing groups, (a|b|c) alternation
- [A-Z] [a-z] \d \s character classes, +*?{n,m} quantifiers
- (?=...) lookahead, (?<=...) lookbehind — match context without consuming it
- (?i) case-insensitive flag

CAPTURE GROUPS ($1) vs FULL MATCH ($0):
- Use $0 in output_template when the entire regex match IS the value you want.
- Use $1 when you need surrounding context to disambiguate — put the value in a capture group.
  With "text": "$1", $start/$end auto-adjust to the group position automatically.

GOOD vs BAD PATTERNS (learn from these):

  BAD:  "\d+"                       — matches every number in the text, most will be wrong type
  GOOD: "(\d+)\s*(?:kg|lbs|oz)"    — only numbers followed by weight units; $1 captures the number

  BAD:  "Alice|Bob|Tokyo"            — memorizes training strings, misses all unseen values
  GOOD: "(?:Mr|Mrs|Dr)\.?\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — captures names after titles

  BAD:  "[A-Z][a-z]+"               — matches any capitalized word (way too broad)
  GOOD: "(?:in|from|at|near)\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — locations after prepositions

  BAD:  "\b\w+@\w+\.\w+\b"         — matches email-like strings but also "user@v1.0"
  GOOD: "(?:email|contact)[:\s]+(\S+@\S+\.\w{2,})" — emails after a label; $1 captures address

  BAD:  one giant regex that handles 5 different sub-patterns
  GOOD: 5 separate rules, each handling one clear case with its own priority

IMPORTANT:
- Do NOT assume entities are capitalized; check the training data.
- Prefer structural patterns (\d+, [A-Z][a-z]+) over memorized alternations.
- When multiple entity types share similar text, use surrounding context to disambiguate.




SCHEMA-AWARE RULES:
For this task, rules must include an output_template that maps matches to the output schema.

Output schema:
entities: List[Entity]

Entity:
  text: string  # The matched text span
  start: integer  # Start character offset
  end: integer  # End character offset
  type: string  # Entity label

Each rule needs:
- pattern: The regex pattern to match
- output_template: JSON template for each match, using variables:
  - $0: Full match text
  - $start: Start character offset
  - $end: End character offset
  - $1, $2, ...: Capture groups (regex only)
- output_key: Which output array to populate (e.g., "entities")

$0 vs $1: Use $0 when the full match IS the value. Use a capture group ($1) when you
need surrounding context for disambiguation — $start/$end auto-adjust to the group.
  Example: "prefix(\w+)" with "text": "$1" extracts only the captured part.

WHAT MAKES A GOOD RULE:
- PRECISION OVER RECALL: A rule that matches 10 things correctly beats one that matches 100 with 20 wrong. Never sacrifice precision for recall. Missing a match is fixable later; a wrong match poisons results.
- GENERALIZE, DON'T MEMORIZE: Rules run on unseen text. Match the *structure* of values, not the exact strings you see in training data.
- USE CONTEXT: The same string can mean different things. Match surrounding words/punctuation and use capture groups ($1) to extract just the value.
- ONE RULE, ONE PATTERN: Multiple focused rules beat one giant regex. Each rule should have a clear, testable reason to exist.
- THINK ADVERSARIALLY: Before writing a rule, ask "what else could this match?" If the answer isn't empty, narrow the pattern or add context.


- Regex patterns (for structured extraction)

IMPORTANT: You must ONLY use the allowed formats listed above. Do NOT generate rules in other formats.

REGEX SYNTAX REFERENCE:
- \b word boundaries, (?:...) non-capturing groups, (a|b|c) alternation
- [A-Z] [a-z] \d \s character classes, +*?{n,m} quantifiers
- (?=...) lookahead, (?<=...) lookbehind — match context without consuming it
- (?i) case-insensitive flag

CAPTURE GROUPS ($1) vs FULL MATCH ($0):
- Use $0 in output_template when the entire regex match IS the value you want.
- Use $1 when you need surrounding context to disambiguate — put the value in a capture group.
  With "text": "$1", $start/$end auto-adjust to the group position automatically.

GOOD vs BAD PATTERNS (learn from these):

  BAD:  "\d+"                       — matches every number in the text, most will be wrong type
  GOOD: "(\d+)\s*(?:kg|lbs|oz)"    — only numbers followed by weight units; $1 captures the number

  BAD:  "Alice|Bob|Tokyo"            — memorizes training strings, misses all unseen values
  GOOD: "(?:Mr|Mrs|Dr)\.?\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — captures names after titles

  BAD:  "[A-Z][a-z]+"               — matches any capitalized word (way too broad)
  GOOD: "(?:in|from|at|near)\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — locations after prepositions

  BAD:  "\b\w+@\w+\.\w+\b"         — matches email-like strings but also "user@v1.0"
  GOOD: "(?:email|contact)[:\s]+(\S+@\S+\.\w{2,})" — emails after a label; $1 captures address

  BAD:  one giant regex that handles 5 different sub-patterns
  GOOD: 5 separate rules, each handling one clear case with its own priority

IMPORTANT:
- Do NOT assume entities are capitalized; check the training data.
- Prefer structural patterns (\d+, [A-Z][a-z]+) over memorized alternations.
- When multiple entity types share similar text, use surrounding context to disambiguate.




SCHEMA-AWARE RULES:
For this task, rules must include an output_template that maps matches to the output schema.

Output schema:

Entity:

Each rule needs:
- pattern: The regex pattern to match
- output_template: JSON template for each match, using variables:
  - $0: Full match text
  - $start: Start character offset
  - $end: End character offset
  - $1, $2, ...: Capture groups (regex only)
- output_key: Which output array to populate (e.g., "entities")

$0 vs $1: Use $0 when the full match IS the value. Use a capture group ($1) when you
need surrounding context for disambiguation — $start/$end auto-adjust to the group.
  Example: "prefix(\w+)" with "text": "$1" extracts only the captured part.

Instructions:
- Add, tweak, or DELETE rules to fix the shown failures and reduce false positives.
- Pay close attention to user_feedback on rules AND task-level USER GUIDANCE — these are direct instructions from the user and MUST be addressed even if there are no failures.
- If a rule has user_feedback, modify or replace that rule to address the feedback.
- IMPORTANT: When updating an existing rule, you MUST reuse the EXACT same "name" as the original rule. Do NOT add suffixes like "_fixed", "_v2", "_updated", etc. The merge system uses name-matching to replace the old version — a different name creates a duplicate instead of replacing.
- If a rule is fundamentally too broad (FP >> TP) and you're providing better, narrower replacements, list the old rule's exact name in "deleted_rules". Only delete if you're providing replacements in "rules".
- If a rule has high false positives (FP >> TP), TIGHTEN its pattern or DELETE it and add narrower replacements. Adding context or narrowing the match is better than piling on new rules.
- NEVER use exhaustive negative lookahead lists like (?!word1|word2|word3|...) with many items. Tighten POSITIVE constraints instead — require stronger surrounding context,
  stricter character classes, or structural anchors. A short positive pattern always beats a long exclusion list.
- Use structural patterns with word boundaries that generalize to unseen text.
- Keep total new/updated rules <= 30.
- Use formats: regex
- Avoid touching unrelated behaviors.

WHAT MAKES A GOOD RULE:
- PRECISION OVER RECALL: A rule that matches 10 things correctly beats one that matches 100 with 20 wrong. Never sacrifice precision for recall. Missing a match is fixable later; a wrong match poisons results.
- GENERALIZE, DON'T MEMORIZE: Rules run on unseen text. Match the *structure* of values, not the exact strings you see in training data.
- USE CONTEXT: The same string can mean different things. Match surrounding words/punctuation and use capture groups ($1) to extract just the value.
- ONE RULE, ONE PATTERN: Multiple focused rules beat one giant regex. Each rule should have a clear, testable reason to exist.
- THINK ADVERSARIALLY: Before writing a rule, ask "what else could this match?" If the answer isn't empty, narrow the pattern or add context.


- Regex patterns (for structured extraction)

IMPORTANT: You must ONLY use the allowed formats listed above. Do NOT generate rules in other formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include Python/code rules. Only return the listed formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include spaCy rules. Only return the listed formats.

REGEX SYNTAX REFERENCE:
- \b word boundaries, (?:...) non-capturing groups, (a|b|c) alternation
- [A-Z] [a-z] \d \s character classes, +*?{n,m} quantifiers
- (?=...) lookahead, (?<=...) lookbehind — match context without consuming it
- (?i) case-insensitive flag

CAPTURE GROUPS ($1) vs FULL MATCH ($0):
- Use $0 in output_template when the entire regex match IS the value you want.
- Use $1 when you need surrounding context to disambiguate — put the value in a capture group.
  With "text": "$1", $start/$end auto-adjust to the group position automatically.

GOOD vs BAD PATTERNS (learn from these):

  BAD:  "\d+"                       — matches every number in the text, most will be wrong type
  GOOD: "(\d+)\s*(?:kg|lbs|oz)"    — only numbers followed by weight units; $1 captures the number

  BAD:  "Alice|Bob|Tokyo"            — memorizes training strings, misses all unseen values
  GOOD: "(?:Mr|Mrs|Dr)\.?\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — captures names after titles

  BAD:  "[A-Z][a-z]+"               — matches any capitalized word (way too broad)
  GOOD: "(?:in|from|at|near)\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — locations after prepositions

  BAD:  "\b\w+@\w+\.\w+\b"         — matches email-like strings but also "user@v1.0"
  GOOD: "(?:email|contact)[:\s]+(\S+@\S+\.\w{2,})" — emails after a label; $1 captures address

  BAD:  one giant regex that handles 5 different sub-patterns
  GOOD: 5 separate rules, each handling one clear case with its own priority

IMPORTANT:
- Do NOT assume entities are capitalized; check the training data.
- Prefer structural patterns (\d+, [A-Z][a-z]+) over memorized alternations.
- When multiple entity types share similar text, use surrounding context to disambiguate.




SCHEMA-AWARE RULES:
For this task, rules must include an output_template that maps matches to the output schema.

Output schema:
entities: List[Entity]

Entity:
  text: string  # The matched text span
  start: integer  # Start character offset
  end: integer  # End character offset
  type: string  # Entity label

Each rule needs:
- pattern: The regex pattern to match
- output_template: JSON template for each match, using variables:
  - $0: Full match text
  - $start: Start character offset
  - $end: End character offset
  - $1, $2, ...: Capture groups (regex only)
- output_key: Which output array to populate (e.g., "entities")

$0 vs $1: Use $0 when the full match IS the value. Use a capture group ($1) when you
need surrounding context for disambiguation — $start/$end auto-adjust to the group.
  Example: "prefix(\w+)" with "text": "$1" extracts only the captured part.

Instructions:
- Add, tweak, or DELETE rules to fix the shown failures and reduce false positives.
- Pay close attention to user_feedback on rules AND task-level USER GUIDANCE — these are direct instructions from the user and MUST be addressed even if there are no failures.
- If a rule has user_feedback, modify or replace that rule to address the feedback.
- IMPORTANT: When updating an existing rule, you MUST reuse the EXACT same "name" as the original rule. Do NOT add suffixes like "_fixed", "_v2", "_updated", etc. The merge system uses name-matching to replace the old version — a different name creates a duplicate instead of replacing.
- If a rule is fundamentally too broad (FP >> TP) and you're providing better, narrower replacements, list the old rule's exact name in "deleted_rules". Only delete if you're providing replacements in "rules".
- If a rule has high false positives (FP >> TP), TIGHTEN its pattern or DELETE it and add narrower replacements. Adding context or narrowing the match is better than piling on new rules.
- NEVER use exhaustive negative lookahead lists like (?!word1|word2|word3|...) with many items. Tighten POSITIVE constraints instead — require stronger surrounding context,
  stricter character classes, or structural anchors. A short positive pattern always beats a long exclusion list.
- Use structural patterns with word boundaries that generalize to unseen text.
- Keep total new/updated rules <= 30.
- Use formats: regex
- Avoid touching unrelated behaviors.

WHAT MAKES A GOOD RULE:
- PRECISION OVER RECALL: A rule that matches 10 things correctly beats one that matches 100 with 20 wrong. Never sacrifice precision for recall. Missing a match is fixable later; a wrong match poisons results.
- GENERALIZE, DON'T MEMORIZE: Rules run on unseen text. Match the *structure* of values, not the exact strings you see in training data.
- USE CONTEXT: The same string can mean different things. Match surrounding words/punctuation and use capture groups ($1) to extract just the value.
- ONE RULE, ONE PATTERN: Multiple focused rules beat one giant regex. Each rule should have a clear, testable reason to exist.
- THINK ADVERSARIALLY: Before writing a rule, ask "what else could this match?" If the answer isn't empty, narrow the pattern or add context.


- Regex patterns (for structured extraction)

IMPORTANT: You must ONLY use the allowed formats listed above. Do NOT generate rules in other formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include Python/code rules. Only return the listed formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include spaCy rules. Only return the listed formats.

REGEX SYNTAX REFERENCE:
- \b word boundaries, (?:...) non-capturing groups, (a|b|c) alternation
- [A-Z] [a-z] \d \s character classes, +*?{n,m} quantifiers
- (?=...) lookahead, (?<=...) lookbehind — match context without consuming it
- (?i) case-insensitive flag

CAPTURE GROUPS ($1) vs FULL MATCH ($0):
- Use $0 in output_template when the entire regex match IS the value you want.
- Use $1 when you need surrounding context to disambiguate — put the value in a capture group.
  With "text": "$1", $start/$end auto-adjust to the group position automatically.

GOOD vs BAD PATTERNS (learn from these):

  BAD:  "\d+"                       — matches every number in the text, most will be wrong type
  GOOD: "(\d+)\s*(?:kg|lbs|oz)"    — only numbers followed by weight units; $1 captures the number

  BAD:  "Alice|Bob|Tokyo"            — memorizes training strings, misses all unseen values
  GOOD: "(?:Mr|Mrs|Dr)\.?\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — captures names after titles

  BAD:  "[A-Z][a-z]+"               — matches any capitalized word (way too broad)
  GOOD: "(?:in|from|at|near)\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — locations after prepositions

  BAD:  "\b\w+@\w+\.\w+\b"         — matches email-like strings but also "user@v1.0"
  GOOD: "(?:email|contact)[:\s]+(\S+@\S+\.\w{2,})" — emails after a label; $1 captures address

  BAD:  one giant regex that handles 5 different sub-patterns
  GOOD: 5 separate rules, each handling one clear case with its own priority

IMPORTANT:
- Do NOT assume entities are capitalized; check the training data.
- Prefer structural patterns (\d+, [A-Z][a-z]+) over memorized alternations.
- When multiple entity types share similar text, use surrounding context to disambiguate.




SCHEMA-AWARE RULES:
For this task, rules must include an output_template that maps matches to the output schema.

Output schema:
entities: List[Entity]

Entity:
  text: string  # The matched text span
  start: integer  # Start character offset
  end: integer  # End character offset
  type: string  # Entity label

Each rule needs:
- pattern: The regex pattern to match
- output_template: JSON template for each match, using variables:
  - $0: Full match text
  - $start: Start character offset
  - $end: End character offset
  - $1, $2, ...: Capture groups (regex only)
- output_key: Which output array to populate (e.g., "entities")

$0 vs $1: Use $0 when the full match IS the value. Use a capture group ($1) when you
need surrounding context for disambiguation — $start/$end auto-adjust to the group.
  Example: "prefix(\w+)" with "text": "$1" extracts only the captured part.
